links.evidence: | title=The Breeding Habits of the Pectoral Sandpiper (Actodromas maculata) |journal=The Auk | publisher=American Ornithologists' Union, etc | volume=1 |pages=218–221 | date=1884 | doi=10.2307/4066833 | jstor=4066833 | issn=0004-8038 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/27446101 | access-date=2025-10-23}} Erolia melanotos | range_map = Calidris melanotos map.svg }} The pectoral sandpiper (Calidris melanotos), often abbreviated pec, is a small, migratory wader that breeds in arctic regions of North America and Asia, wintering in South America and Oceania

links.key: bird-美洲尖尾濱鷸-9cd1e746__distributed_in__loc-薩克拉門托縣-e19a2400
links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: alt=Pectoral Sandpiper|left|thumb|275x275px|At Cosumnes River Preserve in Sacramento County, California. It is a very long-distance migrant, and about half of the species breeds in the boggy tundra of northern Asia, the rest nesting in a range from Alaska to central Canada. The American and most of the Asian birds winter in South America, but some Asian breeders winter in southern and Australia and New Zealand

It is a very long-distance migrant, and about half of the species breeds in the boggy tundra of northern Asia, the rest nesting in a range from Alaska to central Canada. links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: This species also occurs as a regular migrant to western Europe, being seen annually in Ireland and Great Britain. Pectoral sandpiper may rarely breed in Europe in the far north of European Russia and the far north of Norway; vagrant individuals were also found in Scotland in suitable breeding habitat during summer. Many of the birds occurring in Western Europe may be on a regular migration from Asian breeding grounds to winter in Southern Africa. September 2003 saw a record influx to those two countries, with 40 found in Ireland and 150 in Great Britain
